narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 30.50, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both at the 2nd decile—meaning your early grades placed you in the lower part of your class compared to typical students, whose averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 44.5% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. Your undergraduate GPA was the most powerful positive factor, suggesting it gave you a meaningful edge toward passing the bar, as it's above the average of 3.21 for students in the dataset and signals solid college performance. However, your Year 3 school decile rank had the next biggest impact, pushing the prediction toward failure because your 2nd decile position is well below the typical 5.53 average, indicating relative underperformance later in your studies. Your Year 1 decile, also at 2 versus an average of 5.71, reinforced this concern, though with somewhat less weight, highlighting a pattern of lower class standing early on. While factors like your LSAT being below the 36.70 average or your middle income played roles, they weren't the main drivers here. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in undergrad work but lags in school-specific rankings, which the model sees as stronger bar exam predictors. This doesn't define your potential—many in similar situations, including Hispanic students pursuing law full-time, have succeeded by addressing these gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings if possible, or gaining targeted experience like bar prep courses or internships that demonstrate growth. Reapplying after strengthening these areas could shift the model's view in your favor, and schools value persistence from determined applicants like you.
